answers.value:
Our block of North Phelps Avenue between Minnesota Avenue and Mizell Avenue has experienced a noticeable and sustained increase in cut-through traffic and speeding over the past 3–4 years, particularly during weekday morning and late-afternoon commute hours, as well as around school drop-off and pick-up times. Because drivers are using Phelps as an alternative to Orlando Avenue and Lakemont Avenue, vehicles frequently travel at speeds that appear to be well above the posted 25 mph residential limit.

Specific concerns include:
- SPEEDING: Many drivers accelerate aggressively after turning off Minnesota or Mizell, and based on our informal observations (and a few phone-based speed apps), typical speeds are often in the 35–40 mph range. This is especially alarming given that cars are parked along both sides of the street, which narrows the travel lane and reduces visibility.
- PEDESTRIAN SAFETY: Several families on this stretch of Phelps, including ours, have young children who walk or bike to nearby schools and parks. My 10-year-old son and 7-year-old daughter walk to the nearby school bus stop at the corner of Phelps and Minnesota. We have had multiple close calls where speeding drivers did not stop promptly when they saw children stepping toward the crosswalk or crossing from between parked cars. Many drivers also fail to yield at the mid-block driveways.
- NEAR-MISSES & MINOR COLLISIONS: Neighbors have informally documented at least three near-miss incidents in the last six months where drivers had to slam on their brakes to avoid hitting pedestrians or backing vehicles. In late October 2025, a delivery van clipped a parked car on the east side of Phelps while trying to pass another vehicle; although no one was injured, it highlighted how unsafe the current speed and volume are for the width of the roadway.
- SCHOOL-RELATED TRAFFIC: During school drop-off and pick-up times, some parents use Phelps to bypass congestion on the main roads. We often see vehicles rolling through stop signs at Minnesota and Mizell without coming to a complete stop, and idling in front of driveways while waiting for traffic to clear.

As residents, we are not traffic experts; however, we are requesting that the City of Winter Park conduct a formal neighborhood traffic study to collect actual speed and volume data, evaluate sight distances, and determine whether traffic calming measures (such as speed humps, raised crosswalks, additional signage, or curb extensions) are warranted. We want to proactively address these safety issues before a serious injury or fatal crash occurs. We are very willing to collaborate with City staff, participate in community meetings, and help communicate any recommended changes to our neighbors.

persona.background_context:
Laura Henderson is a 39-year-old working mom who has lived at 742 North Phelps Avenue in Winter Park since the summer of 2017. She and her husband, Daniel, bought the modest three-bedroom bungalow when Ethan was a toddler and Laura was pregnant with Chloe, drawn to the tree-lined streets and the ability to walk to nearby parks and schools. Laura works full-time as a senior marketing project manager for AdventHealth in Orlando, splitting her week between two days in the downtown Orlando offices and three days working from the small, sunlit home office she carved out of their former guest room. Her days usually start around 6:15 a.m.; she and Dan tag-team getting the kids up, dressed, and fed before walking them down to the bus stop near the corner of Phelps and Minnesota. On office days, she drops the kids at the stop, then heads down Minnesota Avenue toward I-4, listening to healthcare marketing podcasts or catching up on a true-crime series during the 25–30 minute commute.

Life in the Henderson household is busy but fairly predictable. Ethan, now 10, is in fourth grade and obsessed with soccer and Minecraft, while seven-year-old Chloe alternates between ballet classes and drawing elaborate chalk murals on the driveway. Dan, 41, is a civil engineer for a small consulting firm in Maitland and usually gets home by 5:30 p.m. unless a project deadline is looming. Weeknights often revolve around homework at the kitchen island, quick dinners that Laura plans on Sundays, and shuttling Ethan to soccer practice at Ward Park or Chloe to her dance studio off Aloma Avenue. On Fridays, they sometimes walk with their neighbors—Michael Rivera from 736 and Patricia Nguyen from 751—down to a nearby ice cream shop, turning the slow stroll back up North Phelps into an informal neighborhood catch-up. Their neighbor across the street, Eleanor Brooks at 729, frequently joins them for coffee on weekend mornings, sitting on Laura’s front porch while the kids play with sidewalk scooters.

Over the past few years, Laura has become increasingly protective of the kids’ independence as traffic on North Phelps has grown heavier and faster. She has vivid memories of a near-miss in early 2025 when a car coming off Minnesota Avenue braked suddenly as Ethan stepped toward the crosswalk, and another afternoon when a delivery van clipped a parked car down the block in late October 2025. Those incidents, coupled with regular sightings of cars rolling through the stop signs at Minnesota and Mizell, have made her more vigilant: she now insists the kids pause at every driveway and wear brightly colored backpacks and bike helmets even for short trips. Despite her worries, she loves that Ethan and Chloe can still walk or bike to friends’ houses on the street, and she feels a strong sense of community with neighbors like Michael, Patricia, and Eleanor, who text each other about suspicious cars, stray packages, or lost pets.

Recently, Laura has been juggling a demanding project at work—coordinating a spring 2026 campaign on preventive health screenings—with the usual family calendar full of school events, birthday parties, and pediatric dentist appointments. She’s also trying to be more intentional about her own health, squeezing in early-morning jogs around the neighborhood two or three times a week and attending a yoga class in downtown Winter Park on Saturday mornings when Dan can handle the kids. Financially, the family is stable, though she and Dan are feeling the squeeze of rising insurance premiums and the looming prospect of saving for Ethan’s and Chloe’s college funds. They’ve talked about possibly renovating their small kitchen in 2027, but for now, their priority is maintaining a safe, walkable environment for their children and preserving the close-knit, front-porch culture that first drew them to North Phelps Avenue.

form_summary.form_purpose: Petition form for neighborhood residents to request that the City of Winter Park conduct a traffic speed study on a specified street segment due to traffic or safety concerns.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Staff in the City of Winter Park Planning & Transportation Department, who will review the petition, verify resident support, and decide whether to initiate the traffic speed study.

secrets.detail_content: On the morning of November 6, 2025, around 7:35 a.m., a black SUV sped down North Phelps and did not stop at the Minnesota Avenue stop sign; it came within a few feet of hitting my 10-year-old son Ethan as he was walking from our driveway at 742 North Phelps to meet his school bus for Lakemont Elementary. After that near-miss, the school counselor, Ms. Ramirez, asked Ethan to start checking in with her twice a week because he’s been having anxiety and trouble sleeping about crossing the street in front of our house.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a minor child’s mental health concerns and school counseling arrangements, which go beyond what is necessary to describe traffic safety issues. Sharing Ethan’s name, age, school, and counseling details with city staff in a public-record-eligible petition could expose him to stigma or unwanted attention and reveals private educational and health-related information not needed for a traffic study decision.

places: corner of Phelps and Minnesota Avenue
events: ongoing school counseling check-ins for anxiety

secrets.detail_content: In late October 2025, around 5:15 p.m., a delivery van sideswiped my neighbor Michael J. Rivera’s parked Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps while trying to get around another car that had stopped short. Michael is still dealing with the fallout — his insurance with GEICO raised his premium by about $75 a month because this was his second claim in 18 months, and he’s been arguing with the body shop on Fairbanks Avenue about a $1,200 supplemental repair bill. The constant speeding and tight clearance on our block are what led to this mess for him.

It exposes Michael’s private financial and insurance situation to city staff and possibly the public record without his consent.

secrets.detail_content: Because of the speeding and the way cars whip around the curve between Minnesota and Mizell, my daughter Chloe (7) has been too scared to walk or bike to the bus stop since an incident on September 18, 2025, when a white pickup truck nearly hit her and Patricia Nguyen’s daughter as they stepped out from between two parked cars. Since then, I’ve been leaving work at AdventHealth Orlando early every Tuesday and Thursday at 3:30 p.m. to drive home and walk them to the corner myself, which has already led to a written warning from my manager, Karen Lewis, about my "unreliable availability for late-afternoon client calls."
secrets.why_sensitive: This combines a child’s safety fears with specific employment and disciplinary information, including employer name, manager name, and a written warning. While the unsafe conditions are relevant, disclosing an internal HR issue and scheduling accommodations could affect how city staff view her professionalism and introduces unnecessary workplace privacy concerns into a traffic petition.

places: curve between Minnesota Avenue and Mizell Avenue on North Phelps
events: change in work schedule to leave at 3:30 p.m.

secrets.detail_content: On January 9, 2026, around 6:50 a.m., I had to cancel a follow-up appointment at the Winter Park Women’s Health Center because I was stuck in our driveway at 742 North Phelps while a line of cars used our street as a cut-through from Mizell to Minnesota. A silver sedan stopped directly across our driveway and idled for about 10 minutes waiting for a break in traffic, making me late enough that the clinic charged me a $50 no-show fee and pushed my follow-up for a recent procedure back another six weeks.

The city does not need to know about her women’s health care or billing details to evaluate traffic conditions; including them risks exposing sensitive medical information that could be linked to her identity in public records.

secrets.detail_content: In early December 2025, after yet another near-miss at the corner of Phelps and Minnesota where a speeding car almost hit Eleanor Brooks as she backed out of her driveway, a Winter Park police officer informally suggested to me that if we "really want something done" we should start documenting plate numbers and calling in every incident. I’ve been keeping a running list on my phone of license plates, times, and brief descriptions of drivers’ behavior (e.g., texting, rolling through stop signs), including several parents I recognize from my kids’ bus route and from our church, St. Margaret Mary Catholic, on Park Avenue.
secrets.why_sensitive: This describes quasi-surveillance of specific community members, including identifiable parents from her children’s bus route and church, with license plates and behavioral notes. Sharing this in a city form could strain neighbor relations, expose others to suspicion without due process, and tie her religious affiliation and social network to a complaint in a way that is unnecessary for assessing the need for a traffic study.

artifacts.content:
Hi Ms. Ramirez,

I’ve been meaning to write to thank you for checking in with Ethan so consistently. Ever since that morning on November 6, 2025, when the black SUV flew down North Phelps and barely slowed at the Minnesota stop sign, he hasn’t been the same about walking to the bus. It came within a few feet of him as he was heading from our driveway at 742 North Phelps to the corner, and I think it really shook him more than he lets on.

He tells me he likes talking to you, even if he pretends it’s “no big deal.” The twice‑a‑week check‑ins since mid‑November seem to be helping with the anxiety and the trouble sleeping. He still gets tense when we’re near the corner of Phelps and Minnesota, but at least he’s not waking up from nightmares every night. If there’s anything you’re noticing on your end that we should reinforce at home, please let me know.

We’re trying to keep life otherwise normal for him and Chloe — same routine of walking to the bus for Lakemont Elementary, soccer at Ward Park, and Minecraft marathons on the weekends. I’m also trying not to project my own nerves; between the speeding on our block and that delivery van clipping a parked car down the street last October, I’m constantly on edge when the kids are out front.

Thank you again for looking out for him. It means a lot to us to know he has a calm, familiar adult at school he can talk to.

Best,
Laura Henderson
742 North Phelps Avenue
Winter Park, FL 32789
laura.henderson742@gmail.com

artifacts.content:
Patricia,

Catching up between Zoom calls so this will be a brain dump. I still can’t believe that white pickup on September 18 almost hit Chloe and your daughter when they stepped out from between those parked cars on the curve between Minnesota and Mizell. She still talks about “the scary truck” whenever we walk down North Phelps.

Since then I’ve been leaving AdventHealth Orlando early every Tuesday and Thursday at 3:30 to get home in time to walk them to the bus stop. It’s the only way Chloe will go — she refuses to bike now. Of course, my manager Karen Lewis finally noticed and I got a formal written warning about my “unreliable availability for late‑afternoon client calls.” It’s maddening that the mess on our street is now bleeding into my job.

Meanwhile Michael is still dealing with the fallout from that delivery van sideswiping his Subaru in front of 746 Phelps back in late October — GEICO bumped his premium another $75 a month and he’s arguing with that body shop on Fairbanks over a $1,200 extra bill. Between that, Ethan’s near‑miss with the black SUV at Phelps and Minnesota, and Eleanor almost getting hit backing out of her driveway, it feels like we’re living on a racetrack.

Honestly, we’ve been lucky there hasn’t been a serious crash yet. Mostly it’s just infuriating watching people fly down our block like the 25 mph sign is optional. I swear it’s worse every time I look out the front window.

Anyway, ice cream walk Friday? I could use it.

– Laura

Hi Karen,

I wanted to follow up in writing about the attendance warning we discussed today regarding my leaving the AdventHealth Orlando campus early on Tuesdays and Thursdays.

As I mentioned, since an incident on September 18, 2025, when a white pickup truck nearly hit my 7‑year‑old daughter Chloe and our neighbor’s daughter on the curve between Minnesota and Mizell on North Phelps, Chloe has been extremely anxious about walking or biking to the bus stop. The only way she’ll go is if I’m physically there to walk them from our driveway at 742 North Phelps to the corner by Minnesota Avenue.

Because of that, I’ve been leaving the office at 3:30 p.m. on those two days to get home in time. I understand that this has affected my availability for some late‑afternoon client calls, and I take the written warning seriously. At the same time, I’m trying to balance my responsibilities as Senior Marketing Project Manager on the preventive screenings campaign with keeping my kids safe after a very real near‑miss.

I’d like to explore options that might work better for the team, such as:

• Shifting my in‑office days so that my Tuesday/Thursday departures are on work‑from‑home days.
• Blocking 3:30–4:15 p.m. on those two afternoons as unavailable but making up the time later in the evening.
• Having another PM sit in on calls that fall in that window, with me providing prep notes and follow‑up.

I value my role here and want to make sure we find a sustainable solution. Thank you for your understanding and for being willing to discuss alternatives.

Best regards,
Laura M. Henderson
Senior Marketing Project Manager
AdventHealth – Orlando Campus

Hello,

I’m writing about the $50 no‑show fee that was added to my account for the missed follow‑up appointment on January 9, 2026, at Winter Park Women’s Health Center.

My appointment was scheduled for 6:45 a.m. as a follow‑up to a recent procedure. That morning, when I tried to leave our house at 742 North Phelps, I ended up stuck in our driveway because a long line of cars was using our street as a cut‑through from Mizell to Minnesota. A silver sedan stopped directly across our driveway and idled there for close to ten minutes waiting for a break in traffic. By the time I could finally back out, I was running so late that I would have arrived well after the grace period, and the front desk let me know it would be considered a no‑show.

I completely understand that you have policies to keep the schedule running smoothly, and I don’t make a habit of missing appointments. This one was especially frustrating because it pushed my follow‑up out another six weeks, and I’ve been anxious to make sure everything is healing properly.

I’m hoping you might consider waiving or reducing the $50 fee given the circumstances. I can provide any additional details needed. In the meantime, I’ve already marked the new appointment on my calendar and will plan extra buffer time in case traffic on North Phelps is bad again.

Thank you for your consideration,

Laura M. Henderson
742 North Phelps Avenue
Winter Park, FL 32789

artifacts.content:
Title: Phelps speeding – thoughts & to-dos

I keep thinking about how much our little stretch of North Phelps Avenue has changed since we moved into 742 in 2017. Back then it felt sleepy; now it’s like a cut‑through between Minnesota Avenue and Mizell for everyone dodging Orlando and Lakemont.

Recent incidents I don’t want to forget:

– November 6, 2025 – black SUV blew the stop at Minnesota around 7:35 a.m. and came within a few feet of Ethan as he walked from our driveway to the Lakemont Elementary bus stop. Since then he’s been checking in with Ms. Ramirez twice a week for anxiety and sleep issues.

– Late October 2025 – delivery van sideswiped Michael Rivera’s Subaru in front of 746 Phelps trying to get around a car that stopped short. GEICO raised his premium about $75/month and he’s fighting a $1,200 supplemental bill from the body shop on Fairbanks.

– September 18, 2025 – white pickup nearly hit Chloe and Patricia’s daughter on the curve between Minnesota and Mizell. I’ve been leaving AdventHealth Orlando at 3:30 on Tue/Thu to walk them, which led to the written warning from Karen.

– Early December 2025 – near‑miss with Eleanor backing out of her driveway; Winter Park officer suggested we start logging plates and calling in incidents. Started “Phelps speeding log” note on my iPhone.

If I’m honest, there haven’t been any truly serious crashes right in front of our house yet; it just drives me crazy watching people tear past the kids and not even pretend to respect the 25 mph sign. Most of this is me being a hyper‑vigilant mom, but still.

To‑do: keep noting dates/times, talk to Michael/Patricia/Eleanor about next steps, breathe.

Event: Follow-up – Women’s Health Center (rescheduled)

Date: Thursday, February 20, 2026
Time: 6:45–7:30 a.m.
Location: Winter Park Women’s Health Center

Notes:

• This is the follow‑up appointment that got pushed out six weeks after I couldn’t get out of our driveway at 742 North Phelps on January 9, 2026. That was the morning the silver sedan parked directly across our driveway while using Phelps as a cut‑through between Mizell and Minnesota, and I ended up missing the original 6:45 a.m. slot and getting hit with the $50 no‑show fee.

• Leave the house by 6:15 a.m. at the absolute latest. If traffic is already backed up on Phelps, go north to Mizell and loop around rather than waiting.

• Ask about lingering soreness from the recent procedure, and whether the delay in the follow‑up matters.

• After appointment: swing back home to help Dan with Ethan and Chloe before they walk to the bus stop at Phelps and Minnesota. Double‑check that Ethan is still feeling okay about going on his own some mornings; he’s been better since starting those twice‑weekly check‑ins with Ms. Ramirez at Lakemont Elementary after the November 6 near‑miss with the black SUV.

• If there’s time, quick stop at Publix on the way back for milk, bread, and soccer snacks for Ethan’s practice at Ward Park.

Mike,

I’ve been thinking about what you said on the sidewalk last week about “what’s the point, they already tried to fix Phelps.” I get it. The city put in those speed humps a couple of years ago and honestly they barely made a dent. Half the cars just gun it between them. At this point I’m kind of hoping they’ll consider something more drastic, like making it harder to use our stretch between Minnesota and Mizell as a straight shot cut‑through.

It doesn’t help that you’re still untangling the mess from when that delivery van sideswiped your Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps in late October 2025. Every time I see your car I think about GEICO jacking your premium another $75/month and the stupid $1,200 “supplement” the Fairbanks body shop tried to tack on. The whole thing started because someone was trying to squeeze around a stopped car on a narrow street where everyone is already speeding.

And then there’s everything else: Ethan almost getting hit by that black SUV blowing the Minnesota stop sign on November 6, Chloe and Patricia’s daughter nearly clipped by the white pickup on the curve, Eleanor’s near‑miss backing out of her driveway. No wonder that Winter Park cop told me in early December to start writing down license plates and calling things in. I’ve got a whole log on my phone now.

Anyway, thanks for being willing to sign anything we circulate and for talking to the neighbors at 736 and down by 751. Let’s still do the ice cream walk Friday with Patricia and Eleanor – I need a reminder that North Phelps isn’t just a racetrack.

– Laura

field_coverage.reasoning: The street name is clearly identified as the relevant location of concern. Multiple artifacts describe traffic and safety issues specifically on North Phelps Avenue, e.g., note (index 5): “our little stretch of North Phelps Avenue has changed… now it’s like a cut‑through between Minnesota Avenue and Mizell,” and many others reference incidents on North Phelps. This ties the street name to the petition’s location context.

Minnesota Avenue is explicitly referenced as the cross street bounding the problem area. The note (5) says “cut‑through between Minnesota Avenue and Mizell,” and multiple incidents are located at “the corner of Phelps and Minnesota” (1,3,5,6,7). This clearly establishes Minnesota Avenue as one end of the affected segment.

Mizell Avenue is likewise clearly used as the other bounding cross street. From: Sarah Collins <scollins@cityofwinterpark.org>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@email.com>
Date: 2026-01-22 10:47
Subject: RE: Clarification on "affected properties" definition

Hi Ms. Henderson,

Thanks again for coming to the Transportation Advisory Board meeting last night and for your thoughtful comments about conditions on North Phelps Avenue.

Per your question about how the City defines the “total number of affected properties/lots” for a traffic calming petition:

1. We typically look at the continuous street segment you’re concerned about—in this case, the block from Minnesota to Mizell.
2. We then count each parcel that has direct driveway access to that segment. Corner parcels are counted if their primary driveway is on the petition street.
3. Multi-family parcels (like duplexes) usually count as one “lot,” even if there are multiple units.

You mentioned in your email that your informal count came to **22 affected properties** along your block, including the two corner lots at each end. That number sounds reasonable as a starting point, though it is not the official figure we will use if you submit a petition. Once we receive your completed petition form, our engineering staff will run a parcel analysis in GIS and provide you with the official total.

I know you’re also tracking license plates and driver behavior after the recent near-miss involving your neighbor backing out of her driveway. While that documentation is not required for the petition, it can be helpful context when staff reviews the request.

Please let me know if you’d like to set up a short call to walk through the petition form line by line.

Best,
Sarah Collins
Transportation Planning Division
City of Winter Park
